original_hypothesis.title: _fillQuote Uses Raw approve() Instead of SafeERC20.safeApprove, Causing DoS for Non-Standard Tokens
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: _fillQuote calls `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount))` using raw IERC20.approve instead of SafeERC20.safeApprove. In Solidity 0.8, if the token's approve() doesn't return a value (like USDT), the ABI decoder reverts when trying to decode a bool from empty return data. This would permanently block all claims for that token, as _fillQuote is the only path for LRT claims.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The contract correctly uses SafeERC20 for `safeTransfer` and `safeTransferFrom` throughout, but inconsistently uses raw `approve` in _fillQuote. This code defect breaks the SafeERC20 usage pattern and creates a silent DoS for any non-standard token.
deployment_assumptions: An allowed token must not return bool on approve()
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Target LRTs (weETH, ezETH, rsETH, etc.) are standard ERC20 and return bool. However, allowToken() can whitelist arbitrary tokens post-deployment without validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)); — raw approve, not safeApprove; inconsistent with SafeERC20 usage elsewhere

preconditions: A token that doesn't return bool on approve() is whitelisted
User has locked that token and tries to claim post-conversion

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Raw approve() in require() instead of safeApprove
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Solidity 0.8 ABI decoder reverts if approve() returns empty bytes instead of bool

analysis_questions: Do any current or planned LRT tokens not return bool on approve()?
What happens to user funds if claim permanently reverts? Can they withdraw in emergency?
Is the token's approve non-standard but safeTransferFrom works?
validation_requirements: Test _fillQuote with a mock token that doesn't return bool on approve
Confirm revert occurs in require statement
Verify emergency withdrawal is the only recovery path

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner whitelists a token whose approve() doesn't return data. Users lock the token. After conversion, all claim() calls for this token revert at _fillQuote's require(approve()) line. Users can only recover via emergency mode withdrawal of the raw LRT (not lpETH).

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All locked amounts of the affected token cannot be converted to lpETH
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Emergency mode withdrawal returns raw tokens (not lpETH)

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test with non-standard ERC20 approve behavior

validation_result.mechanism: raw approve() incompatible with non-standard ERC20
validation_result.impact_summary: Using raw `IERC20.approve()` instead of `SafeERC20.safeApprove()` in `_fillQuote` causes a permanent DoS for any whitelisted token whose `approve()` doesn't return a `bool`, blocking all claim operations for that token.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `_fillQuote` function at the `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount))` line uses the raw IERC20 `approve()` call. In Solidity 0.8.20, when calling an interface function declared as `function approve(address, uint256) external returns (bool)`, the compiler generates code that ABI-decodes the return data as a `bool`. If the token's `approve()` implementation does not return any data (as is the case with USDT and some other non-standard tokens), the ABI decoder encounters an empty returndata buffer and reverts. This occurs before the `require()` even evaluates the boolean result.

The contract correctly uses `SafeERC20` for all other ERC20 operations: `safeTransfer` in `withdraw`, `recoverERC20`; `safeTransferFrom` in `_processLock`; and `safeTransfer` for lpETH in `_claim`. The inconsistency in `_fillQuote` is a genuine code defect.

The `allowToken()` function permits the owner to whitelist arbitrary tokens post-deployment with no validation of ERC20 compliance. If a token with non-standard `approve()` behavior is whitelisted and users lock it, all subsequent `claim()` and `claimAndStake()` calls for that token will permanently revert at the `_fillQuote` line, as `_fillQuote` is the only code path for converting non-ETH tokens to lpETH.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the source code. The contract declares `using SafeERC20 for IERC20;` (line 4 import + line 19 using declaration) but does not use `safeApprove` in `_fillQuote`. Instead, line `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount));` uses the raw `approve()`. OpenZeppelin's `SafeERC20.safeApprove()` handles non-standard return values by checking returndata length and content, exactly to prevent this class of issues. The code is defective because it breaks its own SafeERC20 usage pattern at a critical point in the claim flow.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The call `_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)` uses the raw IERC20.approve() interface. Under Solidity 0.8.20, the compiler generates ABI decoding that expects a bool return value. Tokens like USDT that return no data on approve() cause the ABI decoder to revert, permanently blocking the claim path for that token. Should use `_sellToken.safeApprove(exchangeProxy, _amount)` or `_sellToken.forceApprove(exchangeProxy, _amount)` instead.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls claim(_token, _percentage, _exchange, _data) where _token is a non-ETH allowed token
3. _claim() enters the else branch (non-ETH token), computes userClaim, validates data
6. Solidity ABI decoder expects bool return from approve() - if token returns empty bytes, REVERT
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete path is: claim() -> _claim() [else branch for non-ETH tokens] -> _fillQuote() -> _sellToken.approve(). The _fillQuote function is the ONLY path for non-ETH token claims to convert tokens to ETH. If this reverts, the user has no alternative claim path. The user's only recovery is withdrawal via emergency mode (which returns the raw token, not lpETH).

deployment_assumptions_checked: An allowed token must not return bool on approve()
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script (PrelaunchPoints.s.sol) deploys with an empty allowedTokens array: `address[] public allowedTokens;`. All token whitelisting happens post-deployment via `allowToken()`. The script doesn't specify which tokens will be whitelisted post-deployment, so there's no evidence either supporting or contradicting the assumption that a non-standard token could be added.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is straightforward and realistic: (1) Owner calls `allowToken(USDT_ADDRESS)` to whitelist USDT (or any non-standard approve token). (2) Users lock USDT via `lock()` or `lockFor()` before loopActivation. (3) After conversion (convertAllETH), users attempt to `claim(USDT_ADDRESS, 100, Exchange.UniswapV3, swapData)`. (4) The call reaches `_fillQuote` where `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount))` reverts because USDT's approve() returns no data. (5) All claims for USDT-depositing users are permanently blocked. This is a well-documented Solidity 0.8 incompatibility with non-standard ERC20 tokens and is exactly why OpenZeppelin created SafeERC20.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If a non-standard approve() token is whitelisted: all deposits of that token become unclaimable through the normal path. Users cannot convert their locked tokens to lpETH. Recovery is only possible through emergency mode withdrawal, which returns the raw token (not the lpETH the user was entitled to receive). The defect is permanent for the affected token and affects all users who deposited it. The severity depends on whether such tokens are whitelisted, but the code defect exists regardless.
recommended_mitigations: Replace `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount));` with `_sellToken.safeApprove(exchangeProxy, _amount);` or better yet `_sellToken.forceApprove(exchangeProxy, _amount);` (OpenZeppelin v5+) to handle non-standard return values
Consider resetting approval to 0 after the swap completes to prevent residual allowance issues (also addresses INV-STATE-007)
Add token interface validation in `allowToken()` to verify basic ERC20 compliance before whitelisting
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy PrelaunchPoints with a mock non-standard ERC20 token (approve returns no data) in allowedTokens. 2. Lock some amount of the non-standard token via lock(). 3. Have owner call setLoopAddresses, wait TIMELOCK, call convertAllETH. 4. Attempt to claim the non-standard token. 5. Observe revert at the approve() line in _fillQuote. 6. Verify that the same test passes with safeApprove.
uncertainties: The currently intended LRT tokens (weETH, ezETH, rsETH) appear to be standard ERC20 with proper bool returns - the defect would only manifest if a non-standard token is later whitelisted
The exact set of tokens that will be whitelisted post-deployment is unknown
additional_testing_needed: Test with USDT mainnet fork to confirm the revert behavior
Test with a mock token returning empty bytes on approve()
Verify all currently planned LRT tokens return bool on approve()
Test the safeApprove fix to confirm it resolves the issue
